notes: The XML language declaration names Latin and Greek but omits the primary Middle English.
The text preserves manuscript leaf-side milestones 48r-101r and print-page milestones 81-116.

Print footnote information is generally represented inline with CORR, DEL, UNCLEAR, and SUPPLIED; eight NOTE1 elements remain.

bibliographic.citation: A Fifteenth-Century Courtesy Book, ed. R. W. Chambers, and Two Fifteenth-Century Franciscan Rules, ed. Walter W. Seton, Early English Text Society, Original Series 148 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., and Humphrey Milford, Oxford University Press, 1914).

bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 148
bibliographic.title: A Fifteenth-Century Courtesy Book and Two Fifteenth-Century Franciscan Rules

entities.description: Walter W. Seton's portion of a composite EETS volume. The Minoresses component comprises an introduction and bibliography (pp. 61-78), component title and editorial framing, one printed facsimile of Bodley 585 fol. 48r, the rule and Appendix (pp. 81-116), notes (pp. 117-125), and a glossary shared with the Third Order component (pp. 126-127).
entities.label: Seton 1914 edition of two fifteenth-century Franciscan rules
notes: Chambers edits only the opening courtesy-book work; Seton edits both Franciscan components. Seton credits Mrs. Geoffrey Tomes with the Minoresses manuscript transcript and Chambers with helping revise that text.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1914, before the 1 January 1931 threshold stated in the April 2026 U.S. Copyright Office duration guidance; U-M also currently labels the item public domain in the United States.
rights.component: The medieval English and Latin texts, Seton's 1914 transcription and apparatus, and the published fol. 48r manuscript plate

entities.description: The current parchment codex unites two independently produced and donated volumes: unrelated Latin St Albans material at fols. i, 1-47 and the Middle English Minoresses unit at fols. 48-103. They were separately donated in 1604 and bound together after that date.

notes: For the complete host request, specify front and back covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, all end leaves and flyleaves, blanks, edges where feasible, and every manuscript leaf recto and verso; separately identify target fols. 48r-101r so the work is not confused with complete-codex scope.
Ask the repository to reconcile the catalog's fols. 48-103 physical part, inferred msItem endpoint 104, and text ending at fol. 101r before finalizing the imaging inventory.
The current service is free to Oxford members and otherwise costs £1 per scan, averages ten working days, and may decline items for legal, conservation, or other reasons.

physicalDescription.format: Separately produced manuscript codex unit now bound in a composite volume
notes: Seton describes a neat and legible English book hand, finely decorated chapter initials, otherwise black writing, and the same hand through fol. 101r.
The current catalog records parchment and penwork and assigns English origin.
Bodleian/MLGB3 tentatively associates the unit with the London Abbey of St Mary the Virgin and St Francis outside Aldgate on evidence of contents; the question mark is substantive.

entities.description: Source-work context for the main rule only: Urban IV confirmed the Longchamp or Isabella Rule in Religionis augmentum on 27 July 1263; the Middle English witness represents a later revision under Boniface VIII. Seton judges that the English translation probably passed through an unidentified French version.

next_steps: Obtain a written interpretation from U-M before redistributing the XML or a close derivative.
Continue to distinguish copyright status from provider-contract status.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement supersede the 1999 XML restriction against redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian by shelfmark about internal surrogates, conservation limits, complete-codex and work-folio delivery scopes, formats, fees, and item-specific terms.
If ordering new images, request the complete physical host and fols. 48r-101r as separately named scopes and reconcile the 103/104 endpoint discrepancy.
Do not treat Seton's single fol. 48r plate or the complete printed-book scan as a complete manuscript facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Does Bodleian offer an existing complete digital, preservation, or microfilm surrogate of MS. Bodl. 585, and what item-specific access and reuse terms would apply?

next_steps: Search current papal-register and Franciscan corpora for section 1 and compare sections 2-10 with Poor Clare and Friars Minor customaries and ordinals.
Do not add a directed source edge until an exact textual source or securely demonstrated analogue is found.
openQuestions.question: What exact documents underlie Appendix section 1 and sections 2-10, and where, when, and by whom were they translated, composed, and compiled with the main rule?

next_steps: Inspect or obtain images of fols. 101v through the end of the current part and all end leaves.
Ask Bodleian whether physical fols. 48-103, total leaf count 104, and inferred msItem endpoint 104 reflect blank leaves, cataloging convention, or a genuine discrepancy.
openQuestions.question: How should the Part II physical endpoint and the content after the fol. 101r trailer be reconciled across the current catalog and Seton's description?

next_steps: Preserve the current Bodleian/MLGB3 question mark and seek direct codicological, liturgical, obit, and archival evidence.
Review later provenance scholarship before upgrading Seton's contents-based conclusion or his inferred post-Dissolution ownership chain.
openQuestions.question: Was MS. Bodl. 585 Part II copied for or used by the London Minoresses outside Aldgate?

next_steps: Collate the English text with surviving Isabella Rule recensions and French translations.
Retain Seton's French-intermediary judgment as probable, not as an identified source object.
openQuestions.question: Which exact French or Latin version served as the exemplar for the Middle English main rule?

reviewNotes: The cataloged unit is a three-text compilation: the main rule, Appendix papal document, and Appendix observance customary. Same-hand contiguity does not prove one author or one source document.
MS. Bodl. 585 is a current composite codex; only Part II is the source witness. Part I's St Albans material and Sir George More provenance are unrelated to RuleMinoresses.

The XML's 'olim Seton, olim Pennant' note belongs to sibling 3rdFranRule, whose witness is now NLW MS 22873B. RuleMinoresses derives from Bodley 585, as its own title page, Seton's edition, and the current Bodleian catalog agree.
Seton's public fol. 48r plate is partial manuscript-facsimile coverage inside a complete edition scan; it is not a complete Bodley 585 surrogate.
U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement and the restrictive 1999 XML availability notice are preserved as separate rights and provider-terms layers.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned CME-submodule representation of University of Michigan item RuleMinoresses, produced in 1999 by scanning and ScanWorx OCR from Walter W. Seton's contribution to EETS Original Series 148 (1914). Seton's edition transcribes Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 585 Part II: the Middle English Isabella Rule at fols. 48r-72r, an unidentified papal document at fols. 72v-78v, and convent-observance regulations at fols. 78v-101r. The current shelfmark belongs to a composite codex whose unrelated Part I occupies fols. 1-47. The U-M selection retains the component title page and text at printed pp. 81-116 but omits Seton's introduction, bibliography, editorial note, single fol. 48r plate, endnotes, and glossary. A complete public scan of the 1914 book exists; only the printed fol. 48r plate is a public manuscript image, and no complete public Bodley 585 surrogate was found in current official catalog and Digital Bodleian searches.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator; anonymous compiler(s) of the rule, papal fragment, and observance regulations; source rule confirmed by Urban IV and revised under Boniface VIII
catalogSummary.date: Translation and compilation undated; first attested in Bodley 585 Part II, dated c. 1440-1460
catalogSummary.editor: Walter W. Seton (1914); manuscript transcript by Mrs. Geoffrey Tomes; R. W. Chambers assisted revision; U-M/HTI encoding (1999)
catalogSummary.region: England; association of the witness with the London Minoresses is tentative
catalogSummary.title: The Rewle of Sustris Menouresses Enclosid with Appendix (rule-and-observance compilation)

catalogingSubject.label: The Rewle of Sustris Menouresses Enclosid with Appendix (rule-and-observance compilation)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The Middle English compilation delimited by the pinned U-M/HTI XML and Walter W. Seton's 1914 edition: the seventeen-division Isabella Rule at Bodleian Library MS. Bodl. 585 Part II, fols. 48r-72r, followed by an Appendix comprising an unidentified papal document at fols. 72v-78v and convent-observance regulations at fols. 78v-101r. It is not one homogeneous medieval work, not the whole current composite codex, not Seton's complete Franciscan component or EETS volume, and not the sibling Third Order rule whose former Pennant/Seton witness is now NLW MS 22873B.

contentParts.label: The Rewle of Sustris Menouresses enclosid
notes: Each division begins with a decorated or penwork initial in the manuscript according to Seton; the electronic Chapter 1-17 headings are supplied.
contentParts.summary: A seventeen-division Middle English version of the Isabella Rule, revised under Boniface VIII, regulating enclosed religious life from profession through governance and visitation.

contentParts.label: Appendix
notes: Appendix is Seton's and U-M's presentation label. Seton explicitly warns that its contents are not one document.
contentParts.summary: A same-hand continuation comprising an unidentified papal document followed by a distinct body of convent-observance regulations.

contentParts.label: Appendix section 1: unidentified papal document
notes: A reference to Boniface VIII as predecessor proves only that the document is later than his pontificate; Seton found no identification in Potthast or Sbaralea.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English portion of a papal document confirming or modifying the rule, including governance by Friars Minor, common observance, property and exemption provisions, profession, dormitory practice, and enclosure.

contentParts.label: Appendix sections 2-10: convent-observance regulations
notes: Seton treats the shift at fol. 78v ('At alle þe houres') as a document boundary: the following material is not in papal-bull form and later uses first-person plural.
contentParts.summary: Nine sections prescribing bell-ringing, assembly, choir and Mass order, refectory reading and silence, communion, feast observance, table blessings, Holy Week and seasonal rites, and precedence in the liturgical calendar.

dateStatements.display: After the revision attributed to Boniface VIII and no later than the mid-fifteenth-century witness; otherwise undated
notes: The papal revision and witness date supply termini only; they do not date the translation precisely.

dateStatements.display: No later than c. 1440-1460; otherwise undated
notes: The same-hand witness shows the three-unit compilation by the time of copying, not when or where the documents were first assembled.
This dates the surviving copy, not the source rule, translation, or Appendix documents.

extent.description: The encoded content has two DIV1 wrappers and twenty-seven DIV2 divisions: seventeen main-rule chapters and ten Appendix sections. Their numeric labels are U-M-supplied analytic headings, not transcribed medieval rubrics. The BODY contains 59 P elements; the file-wide count of 65 P includes six header paragraphs. It also has 36 print-page milestones (81-116), 107 manuscript leaf-side milestones (48r-101r), 194 Latin and four transliterated Greek FOREIGN elements, eight NOTE1, 21 CORR, 138 SUPPLIED, one ADD, eleven DEL, one UNCLEAR, and no L or LG elements. These are representation metrics, not proof that the source documents were originally numbered in twenty-seven sections.

formStatements.description: All twenty-seven encoded divisions are prose. Embedded Latin and transliterated Greek liturgical formulae do not create verse items; the XML contains no L or LG elements.

genres.label: Religious rule and observance compilation
notes: The whole-scope term preserves the rule-plus-Appendix boundary without treating all three documents as one genre.

genres.label: Vernacular religious translation
notes: The translation relation to the Isabella Rule is secure; Seton's proposed French intermediary is probable rather than identified.

assertion.notes: Seton identifies the form as a portion of a papal bull confirming or modifying the rule but could not identify the bull.

genres.label: Monastic customary
notes: A modern genre label for the regulations governing bells, choir, Mass, refectory rites, feasts, and the liturgical calendar.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML header omits Middle English even though it is overwhelmingly the primary language.

scope.description: Three tagged prayer formulae in the main rule and 191 tagged liturgical formulae, incipits, responses, and office terms in Appendix sections 2-10

languageStatements.label: Ancient Greek

notes: The XML TITLE TYPE=130 value 'Fifteenth-century courtesy book. Selections' belongs to the composite 1914 carrier and is not a medieval title for this compilation.

The XML SOURCEDESC note 'Derived from olim Seton, olim Pennant' is inherited from sibling 3rdFranRule and is contradicted for this item by its own Bodley 585 title page, Seton's separate source discussion, and the current Bodleian catalog. No Pennant or NLW witness edge is asserted here.
The XML language profile declares Latin and Greek but omits primary Middle English; the manifest corrects the profile from direct text and current catalog inspection and normalizes legacy gre to grc.
R. W. Chambers edited the courtesy-book component of EETS OS 148, not the Minoresses component; Seton edited the latter, Mrs. Geoffrey Tomes transcribed it, and Chambers assisted revision.

Copyright, provider terms, physical and reproduction access, the single public fol. 48r plate, the negative complete-facsimile search, and the U-M terms conflict are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.

subjects.label: Religious life of enclosed Minoresses (Poor Clares)
Admission, profession, visitation, discipline, and community governance
Divine Office, choir, Mass, communion, refectory rites, and the liturgical year

summaries.value: A mid-fifteenth-century Middle English rule-and-observance compilation for enclosed Minoresses. Its main text translates the Isabella Rule confirmed by Urban IV and subsequently revised under Boniface VIII; it regulates profession, enclosure, clothing, worship, fasting, communication, sacramental care, offices, visitation, and correction. A following papal document confirms or modifies observance, while nine further sections prescribe bells, choir and Mass practice, communion, refectory blessings, feasts, and the liturgical calendar.

The main rule occupies fols. 48r-72r and has seventeen U-M-supplied chapter divisions. Appendix section 1 occupies fols. 72v-78v and ends with 'without any variaunce or lettinge'; Seton identifies it as part of an otherwise untraced papal document. Appendix sections 2-10 occupy fols. 78v-101r and form a distinct observance customary. The final trailer declares the good work complete.

The current codex is composite: unrelated Latin St Albans material occupies Part I, fols. 1-47, and the cataloged Middle English unit is Part II. Seton prints the compilation at pp. 81-116 and reproduces only fol. 48r as a manuscript plate. The U-M encoding retains a component title page and the full rule-plus-Appendix text but omits Seton's introduction, bibliography, editorial note, plate, endnotes, and shared glossary.

notes: The qualifier makes the exact three-unit boundary visible; neither the manuscript nor Seton gives the whole compilation one uniform medieval title.
titles.value: The Rewle of Sustris Menouresses Enclosid with Appendix (rule-and-observance compilation)

notes: The title is the source-edition and electronic title for the main rule; the inspected fol. 48r plate begins with Urban's address and does not show this heading.
